title: e-mail: rfhuang@mail.ntust.edu.tw
text:
Michael E. Loretero
Rong F. Huang1
Professor
e-mail: rfhuang@mail.ntust.edu.tw
Department of Mechanical Engineering,
National Taiwan University of Science and
Technology,
Taipei 10672, Taiwan, R.O.C.
Effects of Acoustic Excitation on a
Swirling Diffusion Flame
A swirling double concentric jet is commonly used for nonpremixed gas burner applica-
tion for safety reasons and to improve the combustion performance. Fuel is generally
spurted at the central jet while the annular coﬂowing air is swirled. They are normally
separated by a blockage disk where the bluff-body effects further enhance the recircula-
tion of hot gas at the reaction zone. This paper aims to experimentally investigate the
behavior of ﬂame and ﬂow in a double concentric jet combustor when the fuel supply is
acoustically driven. Laser-light sheet assisted Mie scattering method has been used to
visualize the ﬂow, while the ﬂame lengths were measured by a conventional photography
technique. The ﬂuctuating velocity at the jet exit was measured by a two-component laser
Doppler velocimeter. Flammability and stability at ﬁrst fuel tube resonant frequency are
reported and discussed. The evolution of ﬂame proﬁle with excitation level is presented
and discussed, together with the reduction in ﬂame length. The ﬂame in the unforced
reacting axisymmetric wake is classiﬁed into three characteristic modes, which are weak
swirling ﬂame, lifted ﬂame, and transitional reattached ﬂame. These terms reﬂect their
primary features of ﬂame appearances, and when the acoustic excitation is applied, the
ﬂame behaviors change with the excitation frequency and amplitude. Four additional
characteristic modes are identiﬁed; e.g., at low excitation amplitudes, wrinkling ﬂame
with a blue annular ﬁlm is observed because the excitation induces vortices in the central
fuel jet and hence gives rise to the wrinkling of ﬂame. The central jet vortices become
larger with the increase in excitation amplitude and thus lead to a wider and shorter
ﬂame. If the excitation amplitude is increased above a certain value, the central jet
vortices change the rotation direction and pacing with the annular jet vortices. These
changes in the ﬂow ﬁeld induce large turbulent intensity and mixing and therefore make
the ﬂame looks blue and short. Further increase in the excitation amplitude would lift the
ﬂame because the ﬂow ﬁeld would be dramatically modiﬁed. DOI: 10.1115/1.4001768
Keywords: reacting swirling wake, acoustic excitation, nonpremixed ﬂame
1

larger with the increase in excitation amplitude and thus lead to a wider and short

title: e-mail: rfhuang@mail.ntust.edu.tw
text:
Introduction
Swirl ﬂow has been employed to enhance mixing and combus-
tion for industrial gas burners suited for boilers, gas furnaces, gas
turbines, etc. The tangential velocity component imparted by the
swirling ﬂow causes a low relative pressure at the central axis and
thus triggers recirculation at the central axis, which expedites mix-
ing if fuel is spurted as a central jet. However, the swirl number
has to be beyond 0.6, or an annular jet Reynolds number has to be
more than 18,000 to make recirculation possible 1. Many of the
modern day industrial gas burners, particularly for small to me-
dium scale purposes, operate at much lower swirl and Reynolds
numbers.
Downstream recirculation naturally occurs behind a ﬁrmly
mounted blockage disk along the ﬂow stream at the right Rey-
nolds number. Behind such a disk is a subatmospheric pressure
that will entice a recirculation of ﬂuids called the bluff-body ef-
fect. But bluff body offers considerable pressure loss to the ﬂuid
ﬂow, particularly when the blockage size becomes large. A recir-
culation bubble was observed by Escudier and Keller 2 imme-
diately behind the cylindrical center body with a recirculation
zone at the downstream area. The wake is enclosed in a cylindri-
cal tube where the center bodies’ blockage ratios are 0.25 and
0.39. Huang and Tsai 3 used 14 blockage disks to investigate the
recirculation of a swirl ﬂow with swirl number ranges from 0.02
to 0.6. The blockage ratio ranges from 0.026 to 0.563, where the
bluff-body effects were found signiﬁcant if the blockage ratio was
more than 0.11. Several ﬂow structures were found, such as single
bubble, dual rings, and dual rings with vortex breakdown. Huang
and Tsai 4 made a follow-up investigation particularly on the
mixing capability coupled with laser Doppler velocimeter LDV
measurements. They concluded that larger central blockage re-
quires least swirl strength to attain better mixing. However, mix-
ing is not satisfactorily high, particularly with a dual ring ﬂow
structure because the central jet is entangled by the jetlike swirling
ﬂow at the central axis.
An alternative way to improve the entrainment of surrounding
air into the combusting jet is by pulsing the fuel supply. Jet puls-
ing is commonly done either by acoustic excitation 5–15 or by a
valve switching mechanism 16–18. Acoustic excitation is car-
ried out by mounting a loud speaker along the fuel supply line,
while valve switching usually employ a solenoid valve or a rotat-
ing perforated disk to periodically cut the fuel supply. Either way,
investigators reported an ampliﬁed jet exit velocity ﬂuctuation es-
pecially when the pulsation is tuned close to the fuel tube resonant
frequency 5,7–11,15–17. Crow and Champagne 5 deﬁned a
preferred mode of excitation at a Strouhal number of 0.3 by which
the jet response is a maximum velocity ﬂuctuation amplitude. At
an excited central jet Strouhal number of 0.4, Strawa and Cantwell
6 discovered a strong coupling with the coﬂowing annular jet by
which the luminous zone pinches off. Their schlieren images trace
the deceleration of the central reacting jet at such pinch-off point.
The large ﬂuctuating amplitude of the pulsing jet tuned at the
corresponding fuel tube resonant frequency exhibits a dramatic
reduction in ﬂame length 7–9,16. Pulsing a conﬁned jet at a
conﬁnement tube resonant frequency, Parikh and Moffat 17 re-
1Corresponding author.
Contributed by the International Gas Turbine Institute IGTI of ASME for pub-
lication in the JOURNAL OF ENGINEERING FOR GAS TURBINES AND POWER. Manuscript
received August 10, 2009; ﬁnal manuscript received April 20, 2010; published online
August 20, 2010. Assoc. Editor: Thomas Sattelmayer.
Journal of Engineering for Gas Turbines and Power
DECEMBER 2010, Vol. 132 / 121501-1
Copyright © 2010 by ASME
Downloaded 15 Jan 2011 to 129.137.89.240. Redistribution subject to ASME license or copyright; see http://www.asme.org/terms/Terms_Use.cfm

ported that the entrainment at least tripled compared with the un-
pulsed jet. Acoustic excitation was also proven as a powerful tool
to control ﬂame lifting 11,12,14 and ﬂame blow out 15. The
enhanced premixing during acoustic excitation on a lifted ﬂame is
a big help to reduce NOx emission 14. As the fuel jet pressure
varies during the cycle of ﬂow forcing, the fuel outward vortices
collapse into the entrained ﬂow named by Kim et al. 7 as the
phenomena of collapsible mixing. Some of their co-workers 9
made a follow-up investigation on the behavior of ﬂame at feeble
forcing or minimal excitation voltage. It signiﬁes that a burning
jet can be affected by propagating sound waves even at low am-
plitudes. They also found out that in the course of increasing
acoustic forcing amplitude, the fuel vortices change their direction
and cause the ﬂame to elongate. The work of Demare and Baillot
10 concluded that the acoustic excitation transformed the long
and yellow soot radiating ﬂame into a short blue and stable ﬂame.
However, improper acoustic forcing also offers detrimental com-
bustion effects on the likes of ﬂame extinction, ﬂash back, poor
combustion, and system efﬁciency reduction. Baillot and Demare
11 made a classiﬁcation on the behavior of ﬂames at various
forcing frequencies and fuel jet velocities where some illustrate
the adverse effect on combustion.
Nonpremixed combustors are usually involved in industrial ap-
plication for safety reasons in exchange of the low combustion
efﬁciency due to poor mixing. The present combustor is one of
them, as described by Huang and Tsai 4. It is a double concen-
tric combustor where the fuel jet is spurted at the central axis with
an annular swirling air separated by a blockage disk. Inefﬁcient
burning of fuel from this combustor may give rise to carbon mon-
oxide poisoning; thus, improved mixing at the wake is a para-
mount importance. Huang and Yen 19 diverted the central jet
fuel to the annular rotating vortex by installing a control disk at
the fuel jet exit. Indeed, turbulence intensity was ampliﬁed, mix-
ing was improved, and better combustion was attained manifested
by a blue ﬂame. The present work aims to enhance mixing at the
swirling wake by an acoustic excitation of the central fuel jet. The
entrainment of annular swirling air into the pulsing fuel is some-
thing that was rarely found in the literature. Our speciﬁc objec-
tives are i to ﬁnd the best combination of parameters by which
better combustion is achieved and ii to describe the ﬂame be-
havior and mixing phenomena of ﬂow for some of those chosen
parameters.
2

title: e-mail: rfhuang@mail.ntust.edu.tw
text:
Experimental Arrangement
The arrangement of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 1.
A 3.4 kW ring blower with a maximum static pressure of 98.1
bars is used to provide the annular swirling air ﬂow. To minimize
the ﬂow perturbation caused by the rotating blower blades, acous-
tic ﬁlters are used. Air ﬂows are quantiﬁed using rotameters be-
fore they are fed into the test rig. The rotameters are calibrated
using a microﬂow calibration system, and the accuracy is within
1% at full scale. The test rig is a 600400 mm2 circular cylinder
with an internal cavity proﬁled into a nozzle where the contraction
ratio is 9. At the test rig, quantiﬁed air ﬂow passes through a
settling chamber, honeycomb, and mesh screen and is ﬁnally re-
directed by 12 axis-symmetric guide vanes to generate swirl ﬂow.
Guide vanes are proﬁled with NACA 0012 having chord and span
lengths of 60 mm and 50 mm, respectively. Arranged on a pitch
circle of radius 175 mm, the vane angle can be varied from 0 deg
to 70 deg with a minimum resolution of 0.5 deg. Shown in the
subset of Fig. 1 is the test section located at the exit of the test rig
where a 1 mm thick blockage disk is positioned. The exit diameter
Do is 40 mm, while the blockage disk diameter D is 30 mm,
making a blockage ratio B=D/Do2 equal to 0.563.
The central jet is commercial grade propane, which is com-
posed of 95.0% C3H8, 3.5% C2H6, and 1.5% C4H10. This fuel is
stored in an adjacent pressure tank, conveyed by a ﬂexible high
pressure hose where the line pressure can be efﬁciently controlled
by a pressure regulator. The fuel gas goes through needle valves
prior to ﬂow measurements by rotameters. An acoustic excitation
arrangement by placing the loud speaker at the base of the fuel
tube, as shown in Fig. 1, is adopted for the interaction of sound
waves with the fuel jet. This arrangement can also be found in
Ginevsky et al. 20. The 300 mm length nozzle is proﬁled into a
ﬁfth order polynomial where the contraction ratio is 900. A seam-
less stainless steel tube with a length of 600 mm and an inner
diameter of 5.0 mm is connected to the nozzle exit and the block-
age disk. Fuel jet ﬂuid is vented to the wake of the blockage disk
via a 5.0 mm diameter hole d=5.0 mm located at the center of
the blockage disk. Acoustic excitation is carried out by a 15 cm
face diameter loud speaker. The electrical resistance of the voice
coil is 6.6  measured at the loud speaker terminals. The moving
mass of the loud speaker, which includes the voice coil, dust cap,
surround, spider, and diaphragm, is 22.07 g. A square wave signal
is generated by a function generator, and the output signal is veri-
ﬁed by an oscilloscope. The frequency at the function generator is
varied from 10 Hz to 1200 Hz with a minimum resolution of 1 Hz.
The output voltage from the function generator is ampliﬁed by a
power ampliﬁer. Such ampliﬁed signal oscillates the voice coil of
the loud speaker, and the maximum attainable excitation voltage is
25 V. A multimeter is used to measure the root mean square volt-
age across the terminal of the loud speaker.
Image acquisitions are carried out by three different cameras
intended for three speciﬁc purposes. A Sony progressive scan
camera DCR-TRV 900NTSC is used to capture colored moving
pictures for illustrative purposes. IDT X-Stream Vision high speed
camera model XS-4 is used to capture a black and white moving
picture to investigate the evolution of vortices. Nikon still camera
Nikon COOLPIX 990 is used for conventional photography
technique. The detailed operation of each of these image acquisi-
tion tools will be discussed in the following sections.
Jet velocities were measured with a two-component LDV, while
the ﬂow is seeded with 1 m diameter MgO particles. According
to the approximation of Hjelmfelt, Jr. and Mockros 21, the re-
sponse frequency of these particles in a propane medium is 9000
Hz. The blue and green laser beams, which were supplied by a 6
W argon-ion laser Spectra-Physics’ Stabilite-2017, were sepa-
rated and focused through the Dantec Fiber-Flow optical system.
Typical properties of the optical conﬁgurations were listed in
Table 1. The “X” conﬁguration for the two-component light
beams was used. Such arrangement improved the measurement
Fig. 1

Experimental setup

accuracy as both components yield the same values of the jet exit
velocities at the center. A Bragg cell was embedded in the system
to distinguish the directional ambiguity 22. A Dantec ﬂow ve-
locity analyzer FVA enhanced two-component correlation pro-
cessor was used to convert the Doppler signals into frequency
data. The frequency data were fed into a PC-controlled data ac-
quisition system to calculate the velocities and other statistical
properties. The probe is securely mounted on a traversing mecha-
nism where the minimum resolution is 10 m.

title: e-mail: rfhuang@mail.ntust.edu.tw
text:
Results and Discussion
3.1
Flame Modes Without Acoustic Excitation. The swirl
strength of the annular jet is deﬁned by swirl number S, a dimen-
sionless ratio between the axial ﬂuxes of angular momentum to
the axial ﬂuxes of axial momentum 1. From the time-averaged
axial u and azimuthal w velocity components of the annular ﬂow,
the swirl number can be obtained using
S = 
D/2
Do/2
uwr2dr
Dm
2 
D/2
Do/2
u2rdr
1
where Dm is the mean diameter =D+Do/2=35 mm of the
annular jet exit. Measurements and analysis done by Huang and
Tsai 3 on the present geometry of the test apparatus showed that
the swirl number increases almost linearly with the annular Rey-
nolds number for Rea400. However, when Rea is greater than
400, the increasing rate deviates slightly from linearity, which
describes a basic and fundamental feature of a guide-vane swirl
generator 1. Annular jet Reynolds number Rea is the exit Rey-
nolds number of annular ﬂow deﬁned by
Rea = uaDh
a
2
where ua is the volumetric mean axial velocity of the annular
swirling air jet at the exit, Dh is the hydraulic diameter of the
annular jet at the exit, and a is the air’s kinematic viscosity.
Similarly, the central jet Reynolds number Rec is calculated from
Rec = ucd
f
3
where uc is the volumetric mean velocity of the fuel jet, d is the
fuel jet exit diameter deﬁned previously, and f is the fuel’s kine-
matic viscosity.
In the case that no annular swirling air ﬂow is supplied which
is not shown here, i.e., Rea=0, it looks like a typical laminar jet
diffusion ﬂame with a blue ﬂame appeared near the exit region. As
the jet diffusion ﬂame is imparted by swirling annular air ﬂow, the
ﬂame shape is modiﬁed. Typical ﬂame appearances are shown in
Fig. 2a for Rec=2386. It exhibits three distinguishable appear-
ances, which can be found in different regimes characterized by
annular air swirl strength and central fuel jet Reynolds number. As
shown in Fig. 2b, at minimal swirl strength S=0.316, Rea
=364, the bottom area of the ﬂame slightly swells compared with
that of jet diffusion ﬂame. The region where the ﬂame anchors
looks similar to the ﬂame in the wake behind a bluff body
1–4,19 that grows with the increase in swirl strength. It is al-
ways accompanied by ﬂame shortening 1,19, which is also illus-
trated in Fig. 2b. A tarnish and blue ﬂame apparently attaches
the base, which extends to 0.4D. This is the effect of ﬂame
stretching at the base that produces excessive strain and pushes
the ﬂammable layer beyond the lean combustible limit, which
generates local ﬂame extinction 11,12,14. Further increase in
swirl strength to 0.541 Rea=1000 lifted the ﬂame, as shown in
Fig. 2a. Even though the ﬂame is already lifted, its length per-
sists to decrease in all cases of central jet Reynolds number, as
summarized in Fig. 2b. Flame lifting is a common jet diffusion
ﬂame phenomenon achievable by increasing the fuel velocity
11,12,14. But that is not the reason for this kind of ﬂame lifting,
which is inﬂuenced by the annular swirling jet. It is the fuel and
air vortices that move to the same direction, causing poor fuel
dispersion 19. The resulting mixture is not enough to support
combustion at the central jet exit; that is why the ﬂame is lifted.
This mechanism of ﬂame lifting offers a rare opportunity of pre-
mixing in contrast to other works 11,12,14. At the maximum
swirl strength S=0.596, Rea=1700, the base of the lifted ﬂame
moves upstream and reattaches to the blockage disk, as revealed

in Fig. 2a. The short but soot radiating yellow ﬂame, which fully
occupied the top of the blockage disk, signiﬁes a strong recircu-
lation caused by high annular swirl ﬂow. The ﬂame base remains
yellow, but this is the shortest of all ﬂames classiﬁed so far, which
can be seen at the rightmost side of Fig. 2b. Huang and Yen 19
made a similar classiﬁcation except for transitional reattached
ﬂame, which they named as turbulent reattached ﬂame. The two
other classiﬁcations remain the same, such as weak swirling ﬂame
and lifted ﬂame. Flame lengths were measured by a conventional
photography technique. A Nikon still camera Nikon COOLPIX
990 was used to take ten images of the ﬂame with a 1 s exposure
time. This means that for all ﬂame length measurements of the
present work, each data was gathered from a total of 10 s expo-
sure time.
3.2
Flame Modes With Acoustic Excitation. Acoustic exci-
tation triggers the “natural ﬂame” discussed in Sec. 3.1 to exhibit
several modiﬁed ﬂame behaviors at various excitation levels. As
shown in Fig. 3, they are categorized into four groups: ﬂame
modes A, B, C, and D. Apparently, the excitation voltage dictates
the modiﬁcation of the central jet Strouhal number at the fuel tube
exit. The Strouhal number is calculated as
Std = fd
urms
4
where f is the excitation frequency, d shown above is the fuel tube
exit diameter, and urms is the root mean square value about the
time-averaged velocity of the jet during excitation. Excitation fre-
quency is tuned at 180 Hz, while the central jet Reynolds number
is 2386 and the annular swirl strength is 0.316 Rea=364. The
reason behind choosing such excitation frequency and ﬂow con-
ditions will be discussed in detail in Sec. 3.3, together with the
root mean square urms velocity at the fuel tube exit.
As shown in the unexcited ﬂame of Fig. 3a, apparent ﬂame
twisting is visible from 3 to 8D due to annular swirl. Previous
work carried out by Huang and Tsai 23 on the present ﬂow
condition reported that a dual ring with vortex breakdown 1–3
exists at the upstream of such a twisting zone. A rapid drop in the
jet exit Strouhal number from 1.126 corresponding to an unex-
cited case down to about 0.5 is obtained as excitation commenced.
As shown in Fig. 3a, at a jet exit Strouhal number of 0.65
Eexc=0.9 V, the acoustic excitation makes the ﬂame base
wrinkle. An annular blue ﬂame surrounds the core region of yel-
lowish ﬂame. Wrinkling with a blue annular ﬁlm near the ﬂame
base characterizes ﬂame mode A, which shows a somewhat in-
creased combustion capability compared with the corresponding
unexcited case of Fig. 2a. The transition period from ﬂame
mode A to B is manifested by a strong coupling 6 between the
excited jet and the annular ﬂow. Periodic ﬂame structures pinch
off from the wrinkled ﬂame surface through the outer annular ﬁlm
and move upstream as excitation slightly increases. Severe violent
wrinkling motions are very obvious in the downstream area of
ﬂame mode B accompanied by a very obvious nonuniform bulk
expansion around the root area. Oh and Shin 8 spotted seven
symmetric azimuthal structures surrounding the central jet during
acoustic forcing. A similar observation was reported by Demare
and Baillot 10 on such azimuthal structures, which they called
ﬁlament. The highest ﬂame length reduction with a slight change
in Strouhal number in ﬂame mode B, as shown in Fig. 3b, is in
good agreement with the work of Crow and Champagne 5. A
strong response of the jet exit velocity amplitude to a minimal
change in excitation level exists in this ﬂame mode. Increasing the
central j

text:
Results and Discussion
3.1
Flame Modes Without Acoustic Excitation. The swirl
strength of the annular jet is deﬁned by swirl number S, a dimen-
sionless ratio between the axial ﬂuxes of angular momentum to
the axial ﬂuxes of axial momentum 1. From the time-averaged
axial u and azimuthal w velocity components of the annular ﬂow,
the swirl number can be obtained using
S = 
D/2
Do/2
uwr2dr
Dm
2 
D/2
Do/2
u2rdr
1
where Dm is the mean diameter =D+Do/2=35 mm of the
annular jet exit. Measurements and analysis done by Huang and
Tsai 3 on the present geometry of the test apparatus showed that
the swirl number increases almost linearly with the annular Rey-
nolds number for Rea400. However, when Rea is greater than
400, the increasing rate deviates slightly from linearity, which
describes a basic and fundamental feature of a guide-vane swirl
generator 1. Annular jet Reynolds number Rea is the exit Rey-
nolds number of annular ﬂow deﬁned by
Rea = uaDh
a
2
where ua is the volumetric mean axial velocity of the annular
swirling air jet at the exit, Dh is the hydraulic diameter of the
annular jet at the exit, and a is the air’s kinematic viscosity.
Similarly, the central jet Reynolds number Rec is calculated from
Rec = ucd
f
3
where uc is the volumetric mean velocity of the fuel jet, d is the
fuel jet exit diameter deﬁned previously, and f is the fuel’s kine-
matic viscosity.
In the case that no annular swirling air ﬂow is supplied which
is not shown here, i.e., Rea=0, it looks like a typical laminar jet
diffusion ﬂame with a blue ﬂame appeared near the exit region. As
the jet diffusion ﬂame is imparted by swirling annular air ﬂow, the
ﬂame shape is modiﬁed. Typical ﬂame appearances are shown in
Fig. 2a for Rec=2386. It exhibits three distinguishable appear-
ances, which can be found in different regimes characterized by
annular air swirl strength and central fuel jet Reynolds number. As
shown in Fig. 2b, at minimal swirl strength S=0.316, Rea
=364,

3.2
Flame Modes With Acoustic Excitation. Acoustic exci-
tation triggers the “natural ﬂame” discussed in Sec. 3.1 to exhibit
several modiﬁed ﬂame behaviors at various excitation levels. As
shown in Fig. 3, they are categorized into four groups: ﬂame
modes A, B, C, and D. Apparently, the excitation voltage dictates
the modiﬁcation of the central jet Strouhal number at the fuel tube
exit. The Strouhal number is calculated as
Std = fd
urms
4
where f is the excitation frequency, d shown above is the fuel tube
exit diameter, and urms is the root mean square value about the
time-averaged velocity of the jet during excitation. Excitation fre-
quency is tuned at 180 Hz, while the central jet Reynolds number
is 2386 and the annular swirl strength is 0.316 Rea=364. The
reason behind choosing such excitation frequency and ﬂow con-
ditions will be discussed in detail in Sec. 3.3, together with the
root mean square urms velocity at the fuel tube exit.
As shown in the unexcited ﬂame of Fig. 3a, apparent ﬂame
twisting is visible from 3 to 8D due to annular swirl. Previous
work carried out by Huang and Tsai 23 on the present ﬂow
condition r

title: e-mail: rfhuang@mail.ntust.edu.tw
text:
of the fuel supplied. As shown in Fig. 4b, the root mean square
velocity of the ﬂuctuating jet during acoustic excitation reduced
with the increased swirl number. These observations illustrate that
the recirculation at the wake will have a damping effect on the
ﬂuctuating central jet. Off-resonant frequencies should have simi-
lar velocity ﬂuctuation behaviors for the same ﬂame mode. The
exponential proﬁle of the plot is a limiting factor that describes
the behavior of the acoustic generator. Using Eq. 4, the Strouhal
number corresponding to the acoustic excitation voltage is calcu-
lated based on the 180 Hz fuel tube resonant frequency and other
parameters introduced previously. A rapid increase in velocity
ﬂuctuation amplitude is attained near the Strouhal number of 0.3,
which Crow and Champagne 5 described as their preferred
mode. The second and third fuel tube resonant frequencies both
yield strong responses, as shown in Fig. 4b, but deﬁnitely have
different relationships with the jet exit Strouhal numbers and ex-
citation voltages. The range of excitation voltages of such off-
resonant ﬂame modes vary, although the range of Strouhal num-
bers remains the same for a speciﬁc mode of ﬂame, which is
directly inﬂuenced by the jet exit velocity amplitude 5,13. This
can be validated by taking a closer look at Figs. 5 and 6 as the
range of Strouhal numbers are very consistent on various ﬂame
modes at different swirl strengths. A jet exit Strouhal number less
than 0.5 is hardly achieved by those off-resonant frequencies, par-
ticularly at the acoustic antinode 24. For example, at f
=270 Hz only the transition between ﬂame modes A and B exists
at the maximum excitation level. Such transition from ﬂame mode
A to B happened at a jet exit Strouhal number of about 0.5, as
shown in Fig. 3b.
Figure 5 shows the characteristic modes and stability limit of
swirling ﬂame excited at the ﬁrst fuel tube resonant frequency in
the domain of central jet Reynolds number and

title: e-mail: rfhuang@mail.ntust.edu.tw
text:
level gets unstable as it tends to lift from the blockage disk. As
shown in Fig. 3b, a Strouhal number of 0.257 describes a tran-
sition between ﬂame modes C to D.
In contrast to the behaviors of ﬂame shown in Fig. 3a, ﬂames
at Rec1000 will exhibit ﬂame elongation at the peak excitation
level. This is summarized in Fig. 6; i.e., at Rec=246, all variations
of swirl strengths S=0.150,0.316,0.426,0.541,0.596 showed
ﬂame expansion before the ﬂame blows out. However, at a central
jet Reynolds number of more than 1000, all conditions of annular
swirl strength revealed a ﬂame reduction shown in Fig. 6. The
injected volume of fuel in every acoustic cycle for ﬂames at Rec
1000 is proportionally larger than its total ﬂame size compared
with ﬂames at Rec 1000. Thus, the injection time for Rec
1000 relative to its volume is longer than the Rec 1000 ﬂames.
The long injection time will yield a long and cigarlike ﬂame,
while the shorter injection time guarantees a short pufﬂike buoyant
ﬂame, as deﬁned and described by Hermanson et al. 18. At
Rec 1000, a least amount of swirl strength has the most reduc-
tion in ﬂame length before it blows out. This is due to their dif-
ferences in the amount of excitation level before ﬂame blows out
or reaches the maximum excitation of 25 V. For example, let us
compare the ﬂame reduction of the two swirl strengths, namely,
0.316 Rea=364 and 0.541 Rea=1000, at the same Rec of 2386.
Figure 6 shows an Lf/Lo value for S=0.316 and 0.541 equal to
0.37 and 0.66, respectively. This means that a swirl strength of
0.316 Rea=364 reduces 63% of its original and unexcited ﬂame
length before it blows out, while 0.541 Rea=1000 only reduces
34% at the peak excitation level. In Fig. 3, at Rec=2386 and S
=0.316 Rea=364, the ﬂame blows out when the Strouhal num-
ber is less than 0.166 Eexc15 V. In Fig. 5, at S=0.541 Rea
=1000, the ﬂame survives the maximum excitation correspond-
ing to the Strouhal number of approximately 0.308. Thus, for a

title: e-mail: rfhuang@mail.ntust.edu.tw
text:
processing of this jet vortex necessarily wrinkled the ﬂame sur-
face at ﬂame mode A, as discussed in Sec. 3.2. A larger amount of
annular air is entrained and causes some ﬂame structures to pinch
off at near downstream sections 6. Previous work 3,4,23 illus-
trated a dual ring setting in the blockage disk: an outer ring, which
is presently labeled as a recirculation bubble, and an inner ring,
which decomposes into a radially moving mass due to acoustic
pulsing. As shown in Fig. 7b and elucidated in Fig. 7c, a radi-
ally moving mass cannot be transformed into an inner vortex ring
because it is consumed by the central fuel jet during the positive
half of the acoustic cycle. The radially moving mass absorbed the
heat radiated to the blockage disk and consequently preheated the
oxidizing air. This moving mass is displaced by the fuel vortex
during the second and third quarter of the acoustic cycle. At this
period, a large quantity of ﬂuid escaped through the upper sepa-
ration point of the recirculation bubble. These periodic exchanges
of momentum, accelerating and decelerating the central fuel jet,
substantially enhanced turbulence and mixing 13. As the fuel
vortex left from the jet exit during the fourth to the ﬁrst quarter of
the next acoustic cycle, the radially moving mass is entangled by
the central jet through the lower separation point. At this instant,
the lower separation point of the recirculation bubble displaced a
large quantity of preheated oxidizing gas to the central jet, which
is very useful for spontaneous combustion.
Figure 8 shows how the ﬂow structure is dramatically altered
by a large acoustic excitation level. The chosen images of ﬂow in
Figs. 8a–8c are taken during the third quarter of the acoustic
cycle where the fuel vortex has totally ejected but is still visually
identiﬁable. The region of interest in each of the colored pictures
is zoomed out by the corresponding images from the black and
white pictures taken by a high speed c

title: e-mail: rfhuang@mail.ntust.edu.tw
text:
on top of the blockage disk. The periodic ﬂaming of the recircu-
lation bubble made the total reaction zone expanded at its base
described in Sec. 3.2 as ﬂame mode B. The radially moving mass,
however, disappeared because it is totally consumed by the central
jet as the pulsing amplitude necessarily escalates the entrainment
of such surrounding gas 7,16,17. Entrainment fathomed down to
the fuel tube as excitation increased and tuned the ﬂame to mode
C. Figure 8b is an example of ﬂame and ﬂow interactions that
caused the ﬂame base to be diverged. The central jet Strouhal
number is 0.29, which represents ﬂame mode C. The roll-up of
vortex shown in Fig. 8b is caused by the entrainment momen-
tum, which is pushed by the incoming acceleration of the fuel jet.
In the case of pure jet excitation where ﬂame is not stabilized by
swirl ﬂow, the rolling up of this vortex will block the entrainment
of surrounding air, causing the ﬂame to elongate 8. For the case
of the present work, certainly it moved opposite with the rim of
the recirculation bubble catching a sufﬁcient amount of oxidizing
air and preventing the ﬂame from elongation. However, the effect
remains obvious as the ﬂame length reduction normalized com-
pared with ﬂame modes A and B described in Sec 3.2. The roll-up
vortex is initially composed of a lean mixture left behind from the
previous acoustic cycle. The strong magnitude of the recirculation
bubble shattered the upward rotating vortex, generating another
roll-down vortex behind it, as shown in the elucidation of 	
=280 deg in Fig. 8b. This back-to-back and double rolled cen-
tral vortex arose at approximately the same level with the upper
separation point of the recirculation bubble in the downstream
area. Some portions of the burned gases, together with the pre-
heated circulating air at the recirculation bubble, are sucked back
to the fuel tube and ignited the collapsible mixing zone. Increas-
ing the excitation level entice more gas to be entraine

title: e-mail: rfhuang@mail.ntust.edu.tw
text:
Conclusions
A nonpremixed reacting swirling wake has been excited acous-
tically at various ranges of excitation levels and frequencies. The
fuel tube resonance frequency was experimentally obtained at 180
Hz and its harmonics by which minimal excitation level can
modify the ﬂame. Velocity at the jet exit was measured by two-
component LDV at the ﬁrst fuel tube resonant frequency. The
Strouhal number of the acoustic excitation was calculated based
on the ﬁrst fuel tube resonant frequency and the root mean square
of the ﬂuctuating velocity at the central jet exit. Swirl strength
substantially damped the jet velocity ﬂuctuation and at the same
time mitigated the reduction in ﬂame length. In the summarized
representation of ﬂame reduction, the forcing system effectively
reduced the ﬂame length at Rec=2386. Flammability and stability
chart illustrated that ﬂame stabilization is weak at a swirl strength
close to 0.491 Rea=728.
The ﬂame at the swirling wake exhibited four different ﬂame
behaviors during acoustic excitation classiﬁed into ﬂame modes
A, B, C, and D. The mechanics of mixing at each ﬂame mode was
discerned from phase locked Mie scattering images captured by a
high speed camera. At mode A, low excitation amplitudes induce
beadlike vortices in the central fuel jet and make the ﬂame
wrinkles. At mode B, the central jet vortices become larger with
the increase in excitation amplitude and thus lead to a wider and
shorter ﬂame. At mode C, the excitation amplitude is increased
further to exceed the critical value so that the central jet vortices
change the rotation direction and pacing with the annular jet vor-
tices. Large turbulent intensity and mixing are induced due to the
drastic change in ﬂow ﬁeld and therefore make the ﬂame looks
blue and short. At mode D, a lifted ﬂame is formed because the
excitation amplitude is large enough to modify a strong but small
recirculation bubble sitting on the disk.

title: e-mail: rfhuang@mail.ntust.edu.tw
text:
Conclusions
A nonpremixed reacting swirling wake has been excited acous-
tically at various ranges of excitation levels and frequencies. The
fuel tube resonance frequency was experimentally obtained at 180
Hz and its harmonics by which minimal excitation level can
modify the ﬂame. Velocity at the jet exit was measured by two-
component LDV at the ﬁrst fuel tube resonant frequency. The
Strouhal number of the acoustic excitation was calculated based
on the ﬁrst fuel tube resonant frequency and the root mean square
of the ﬂuctuating velocity at the central jet exit. Swirl strength
substantially damped the jet velocity ﬂuctuation and at the same
time mitigated the reduction in ﬂame length. In the summarized
representation of ﬂame reduction, the forcing system effectively
reduced the ﬂame length at Rec=2386. Flammability and stability
chart illustrated that ﬂame stabilization is weak at a swirl strength
close to 0.491 Rea=728.
The ﬂame at the swirling wake exhibited four different ﬂame
behaviors during acoustic excitation classiﬁed into ﬂame modes
A, B, C, and D. The mechanics of mixing at each ﬂame mode was
discerned from phase locked Mie scattering images captured by a
high speed camera. At mode A, low excitation amplitudes induce
beadlike vortices in the central fuel jet and make the ﬂame
wrinkles. At mode B, the central jet vortices become larger with
the increase in excitation amplitude and thus lead to a wider and
shorter ﬂame. At mode C, the excitation amplitude is increased
further to exceed the critical value so that the central jet vortices
change the rotation direction and pacing with the annular jet vor-
tices. Large turbulent intensity and mixing are induced due to the
drastic change in ﬂow ﬁeld and therefore make the ﬂame looks
blue and short. At mode D, a lifted ﬂame is formed because the
excitation amplitude is large enough to modify a strong but small
recirculation bubble sitting on the disk.

text:
References
1 Gupta, A. K., Lilley, D. G., and Syred, N., 1984, Swirl Flows, Abacus, Cam-
bridge, pp. 13–117.
2 Escudier, M. P., and Keller, J. J., 1985, “Recirculation in Swirling Flow: A
Manifestation of Vortex Breakdown,” AIAA J., 23, pp. 111–116.
3 Huang, R. F., and Tsai, F. C., 2001, “Observations of Swirling Flows Behind
Circular Discs,” AIAA J., 39, pp. 1106–1112.
4 Huang, R. F., and Tsai, F. C., 2001, “Flow Field Characteristics of Swirling
Double Concentric Jets,” Exp. Therm. Fluid Sci., 25, pp. 151–161.
5 Crow, S. C., and Champagne, F. H., 1971, “Orderly Structure in Jet Turbu-
lence,” J. Fluid Mech., 48, pp. 547–591.
6 Strawa, A. W., and Cantwell, B. J., 1985, “Visualization of the Structure of a
Pulsed Methane/Air Diffusion Flame,” Phys. Fluids, 28, pp. 2317–2320.
7 Kim, T. K., Park, J., and Shih, H. D., 1993, “Mixing Mechanism Near the
Nozzle Exit in a Tone Excited Non-Premixed Jet Flame,” Combust. Sci. Tech-
nol., 89, pp. 83–100.
8 Oh, S. K., and Shin, H. D., 1998, “A Visualization Study on the Effect of
Forcing Amplitude on Tone-excited Isothermal Jets and Jet Diffusion Flames,”
Int. J. Energy Res., 22, pp. 343–354.
9 Lee, K. M., Kim, T. K., Kim, W. J., Kim, S. G., Park, J., and Keel, S. N., 2002,
“A Visual Study on Flame Behavior in Tone-Excited Non-Premixed Jet
Flames,” Fuel, 81, pp. 2249–2255.
10 Demare, D., and Baillot, F., 2004, “Acoustic Enhancement of Combustion in
Lifted Non-Premixed Jet Flames,” Combust. Flame, 139, pp. 312–328.
11 Baillot, F., and Demare, D., 2002, “Physical Mechanisms of Lifted Non-
Premixed Flame Stabilized in an Acoustic Field,” Combust. Sci. Technol.,
174, pp. 73–98.
12 Chao, Y. C., Wu, C. Y., Yuan, T., and Cheng, T. S., 2002, “Stabilization
Process of a Lifted Flame Tuned by Acoustic Excitation,” Combust. Sci. Tech-
nol., 174, pp. 87–110.
13 Lovett, J. A., and Turns, S. R., 1990, “Experiments on Axisymmetrically
Pulsed Turbulent Jet Flames,” AIAA J., 28, pp. 38–46.
14 Chao, Y. C., Yuan, T., and Tseng, C. S., 1996, “Effects of Flame Lifting and
Acoustic Excitation on the Reduction of NOx Emissions,” Combust. Sci. Tech-
nol., 113, pp. 49–65.
15 Wu, C. Y., Chao, Y. C., Cheng, T. S., Li, Y. H., Lee, K. Y., and Yuan, T., 2006,
“The Blowout Mechanism of Turbulent Jet Diffusion Flames,” Combust.
Flame, 145, pp. 481–494.
